title: Opioid Overdose with Naloxone Titration
subtitle: Toxicology Emergency & Re-Sedation Risk Management

patient.name: S. Reyes
patient.admitDate: Today, 1030 (EMS transport, found unresponsive at home)

labs.v: Opiates +, Fentanyl +, Benzodiazepines neg, Cocaine neg, Amphetamines neg
Sinus bradycardia 58, prolonged QTc 482 ms, no ST changes
Bilateral fluffy patchy infiltrates in lower lobes; no focal consolidation; cardiac silhouette normal

nursesNote: 1100 — 28 y/o female brought in via EMS at 1030 after being found unresponsive in her apartment by her roommate at approximately 1000. PMH: chronic low back pain following MVA 3 years ago, major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder. Medication list per pharmacy records: oxycodone-acetaminophen 10/325 mg q6h PRN (last refill 8 days ago, prescription not renewed despite multiple voicemails to pain clinic). Roommate reports client 'has been really stressed about money' and was seen taking 'a blue pill that wasn't her usual one' approximately 2 hours before being found unresponsive. Empty water bottles and prescription pill bottles at the scene. EMS found client unresponsive with RR 4/min, occasional gasping respirations, pinpoint pupils (1 mm bilaterally), mottled extremities, initial scene SpO2 72% on RA. EMS administered naloxone 4 mg intranasal with partial response: RR improved to 12, SpO2 to 90% on NRB, pupils dilated slightly to 3 mm, but client remained obtunded; second 2 mg dose IN administered en route. On ED arrival 1030: client lethargic, withdrawing to painful stimuli, not verbal. 1100 reassessment: client increasingly obtunded, RR has decreased to 8/min with apneic pauses, SpO2 down to 86% on 4L NC — RE-SEDATING as naloxone wears off. New crackles auscultated at bilateral lung bases. CXR consistent with noncardiogenic pulmonary edema. UDS positive for opiates AND fentanyl (consistent with the 'blue pill' being counterfeit fentanyl-laced pressed pill). POC glucose 84 — rules out hypoglycemia. CK 4,200 — rhabdomyolysis from prolonged immobility on floor. Roommate at bedside, very distressed; reports she has tried multiple times to get client into treatment.
providerOrders: Activate ED Code; prepare for possible intubation if respiratory drive does not recover with naloxone
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, continuous end-tidal CO2 (capnography)
Naloxone 0.4 mg IV push — TITRATE: repeat q2–3 min to RR >12 and adequate oxygenation; goal is restoration of respiratory drive, NOT full reversal
If respiratory depression recurs after 2–3 boluses: initiate naloxone IV infusion at ~2/3 of the cumulative effective bolus dose per hour
Bag-mask ventilation if RR <8/min or apneic
Supplemental O2 via NRB; titrate to SpO2 >92%
Establish/confirm two large-bore IV access points (or IO if needed)
0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus, then 200 mL/hr; target UOP >1 mL/kg/hr for rhabdomyolysis prevention
STAT BMP, CBC, ABG, lactate, CK, troponin, LFTs, acetaminophen, salicylate, UDS, ETOH (if not already drawn)
12-lead ECG; serial ECGs given prolonged QT
Insert indwelling Foley catheter; monitor for myoglobinuria (tea-colored urine)
Observation in monitored bed for minimum 4–6 hours after last naloxone dose (longer for fentanyl/methadone — up to 12–24 hours)
Once stable: psychiatry consult, social work consult for MAT (medication-assisted treatment) referral
Discharge planning: take-home naloxone (Narcan nasal spray) with education, MAT bridge (buprenorphine) coordination, follow-up with outpatient addiction medicine
DO NOT administer activated charcoal (ineffective for parenteral opioids, aspiration risk in obtunded client)
DO NOT administer flumazenil (no role in opioid overdose; risk of seizure)
Use person-first language: avoid stigmatizing terms

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: RR 8/min with apneic pauses, SpO2 dropping to 86%, increasing obtundation — RE-SEDATING after initial naloxone response
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — most immediately life-threatening. Re-sedation occurs because naloxone has a shorter half-life (~30–90 min) than most opioids, especially fentanyl, methadone, and extended-release formulations. Without continued reversal, the client will progress to respiratory arrest.

opts.text: ABG pH 7.21, PaCO2 68 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg — acute respiratory acidosis with hypoxemia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms inadequate ventilation from respiratory depression. Rising CO2 confirms the lungs cannot clear it. PaO2 58 with supplemental O2 confirms inadequate gas exchange. This is type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure — driven by the opioid-induced hypoventilation.

opts.text: Pinpoint pupils on initial assessment (1 mm bilaterally) that dilated to 3 mm after naloxone
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — part of the classic opioid toxidrome (CNS depression + respiratory depression + miosis). Response to naloxone with pupil dilation confirms opioid as the cause. Pupil size is a useful continuing assessment of opioid effect — pinpoint = opioid effect; dilation = reversal.

opts.text: UDS positive for opiates AND fentanyl (counterfeit fentanyl-laced pill)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — explains the severity and informs monitoring duration. Illicit fentanyl ('blues,' 'M30s') is now responsible for the majority of US overdose deaths. Fentanyl is 50–100x more potent than morphine and has a longer duration than naloxone, requiring extended monitoring (12–24 hours typical) and often a naloxone infusion.

opts.text: Bilateral fluffy infiltrates on CXR with new crackles
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — noncardiogenic pulmonary edema. Can occur from the overdose itself (hypoxic capillary leak) or be precipitated by naloxone reversal (sympathetic surge, negative-pressure pulmonary edema). Treat with supplemental O2, PEEP if intubated, supportive care; usually resolves over 24–48 hours.

opts.text: CK 4,200 U/L (rhabdomyolysis from prolonged immobility)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Hours of immobility compress muscle compartments, releasing myoglobin which can precipitate in renal tubules and cause AKI. Aggressive IV fluids (target UOP >1 mL/kg/hr) is the cornerstone of prevention. Watch for hyperkalemia from muscle breakdown.

opts.text: History of recent unauthorized street drug use ('blue pill that wasn't her usual one')
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — establishes the exposure. Pharmacy records show legitimate prescription ran out 8 days ago. Street pills marketed as oxycodone are now overwhelmingly counterfeit pressed fentanyl. This information drives the clinical decision around extended monitoring and harm reduction interventions.

opts.text: Roommate reports client has been depressed and financially stressed; multiple unsuccessful attempts to get her into treatment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial context informs safe disposition. Depression with substance use raises concern for intentional vs unintentional overdose. Once medically stabilized, psychiatric evaluation is essential. Harm reduction (take-home naloxone, MAT referral) reduces mortality even when treatment isn't immediately accepted.

opts.text: Prolonged QTc 482 ms on ECG
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Methadone and high-dose opioid use can prolong QT; risk of torsades de pointes. Monitor for arrhythmia; correct electrolyte abnormalities (especially K+ and Mg++); avoid other QT-prolonging agents (ondansetron, haloperidol) when possible.

opts.text: POC glucose 84 mg/dL
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue — but importantly RULES OUT hypoglycemia as a competing cause of altered mental status. Always check glucose in any unresponsive client (the 'don't ever forget glucose' rule).

opts.text: Acetaminophen and salicylate levels within normal limits
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue — important rule-OUT findings. Co-ingestion of acetaminophen (from combo products like Percocet) at toxic doses requires N-acetylcysteine. Both negative here, so no additional toxicology intervention needed beyond opioid reversal.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Opioid binding to mu receptors in the brainstem suppresses the respiratory drive, producing the classic triad of CNS depression, respiratory depression, and miosis
opts.rat: CORRECT — the defining mechanism. Mu receptor activation in the medullary respiratory centers blunts the CO2 response curve. Miosis is from mu receptor activation in the Edinger-Westphal nucleus. This triad in the appropriate context is opioid toxidrome until proven otherwise.

opts.text: Re-sedation is occurring because naloxone's half-life (~30–90 min) is shorter than fentanyl's effective duration (often >2–4 hours in overdose), so naloxone wears off while the opioid is still active
opts.rat: CORRECT — the critical pharmacokinetic principle that drives monitoring decisions. This is why the client needs either repeat naloxone doses or a continuous infusion AND extended observation (12–24 hours typical for fentanyl, longer for methadone, extended-release formulations).

opts.text: Naloxone is a competitive mu opioid receptor antagonist that displaces the opioid from receptors, rapidly reversing CNS and respiratory depression
opts.rat: CORRECT. Acts within 1–2 min IV, 2–5 min IN. The goal is to RESTORE adequate respiratory drive (RR >12, adequate SpO2), NOT to fully wake the client. Aggressive over-reversal precipitates withdrawal in dependent clients.

opts.text: Large bolus IV naloxone in an opioid-dependent client precipitates acute withdrawal: agitation, vomiting (aspiration risk), tachycardia, hypertension, possible seizures
opts.rat: CORRECT — HIGH-YIELD NCLEX TRAP. The titration approach (small doses, 0.04–0.4 mg IV q2–3 min) avoids this complication. Large empiric doses (2 mg or 4 mg) given to a known dependent client can be dangerous and counterproductive. Goal: restore breathing, not full reversal.

opts.text: Noncardiogenic pulmonary edema in opioid overdose is from a combination of hypoxic capillary leak and (after naloxone) negative-pressure pulmonary edema when the client suddenly inspires deeply against partially obstructed airways
opts.rat: CORRECT. Pathophysiology is complex and not fully understood. Management is supportive: supplemental oxygen, PEEP if intubated, monitor and correct hypoxemia. Usually resolves over 24–48 hours.

opts.text: Rhabdomyolysis is from prolonged immobility compressing muscle compartments; the released myoglobin can precipitate in renal tubules and cause AKI
opts.rat: CORRECT. Aggressive IV crystalloid (target UOP >1 mL/kg/hr) dilutes and excretes myoglobin before it can precipitate. Monitor potassium (released from muscle), CK trend, urine for tea-colored discoloration, and creatinine.

opts.text: Administering activated charcoal would effectively decontaminate the opioid given the recent ingestion
opts.rat: INCORRECT — and dangerous. Activated charcoal is contraindicated in clients with depressed mental status (aspiration risk) and is ineffective for parenteral or rapidly absorbed opioids. Decontamination is rarely indicated in opioid overdose; reversal with naloxone and supportive care is the standard.

opts.text: Flumazenil should be administered alongside naloxone to ensure full reversal of all sedating effects
opts.rat: INCORRECT. Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine antagonist — has no role in pure opioid overdose. Its use in mixed overdoses is also discouraged because it can precipitate seizures in benzodiazepine-dependent clients. Stick with naloxone titration.

opts.text: Once the client wakes up and is talking, she can be discharged home immediately from the ED
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — DANGEROUS. The re-sedation risk extends well beyond the initial naloxone response. Minimum 4–6 hours observation after the last naloxone dose; 12–24 hours for fentanyl-positive overdoses; up to 48 hours for methadone or extended-release opioids. Premature discharge has caused fatal re-sedations after the client returns home.

opts.text: The client should be addressed using person-first language ('person with opioid use disorder') rather than stigmatizing terms ('addict', 'junkie')
opts.rat: CORRECT — supported by NCSBN and addiction medicine guidelines. Stigmatizing language reduces treatment engagement, worsens outcomes, and is unprofessional. Person-first language ('person with opioid use disorder,' 'substance misuse,' 'positive UDS') reflects current standards.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Respiratory Arrest r/t opioid-induced respiratory depression and re-sedation as naloxone wears off
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Airway/Breathing (ABCs). RR 8 with apneic pauses and SpO2 86% is impending respiratory failure. Without continued naloxone (bolus or infusion), the client will progress to apnea, anoxic brain injury, and arrest within minutes.

opts.text: Impaired Gas Exchange r/t acute respiratory acidosis (PaCO2 68, PaO2 58) and noncardiogenic pulmonary edema
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Even with naloxone restoring respiratory drive, the pulmonary edema and acidosis must be corrected. Supplemental O2, PEEP if intubated, and ongoing ventilation are required. Hypoxia for >5 min can cause irreversible brain injury.

opts.text: Risk for AKI r/t rhabdomyolysis from prolonged immobility
opts.rat: #3 — Important. CK 4,200 indicates active muscle breakdown. IV fluids with target UOP >1 mL/kg/hr prevent myoglobin precipitation. Watch for hyperkalemia from muscle release. Worsens prognosis if AKI requires dialysis.

opts.text: Risk for Suicidal Behavior / Continued Substance Use r/t depression, untreated chronic pain, and lapsed treatment plan
opts.rat: #4 — Critical for safe disposition but not immediately life-threatening once medically stable. Psychiatry evaluation, MAT referral, take-home naloxone, and outpatient bridging are all addressed once the acute crisis resolves. Without these, recurrence and mortality are high.

opts.text: Anxiety r/t emergency hospitalization, family witness to overdose, and stigma
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment but real. Compassionate, non-judgmental communication once awake is critical to treatment engagement. The roommate is also experiencing acute distress and needs support and education (including take-home naloxone training).

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Naloxone 0.4 mg IV push, repeat q2–3 min titrated to RR >12 and adequate oxygenation
opts.rat: CORRECT titration approach. Goal is to RESTORE RESPIRATORY DRIVE, not full reversal. Smaller starting doses (0.04 mg) may be used in known opioid-dependent clients to minimize withdrawal; standard 0.4 mg IV is appropriate when status is unclear or for initial dose.

opts.text: Initiate naloxone IV infusion at approximately 2/3 of the effective cumulative bolus dose per hour once respiratory drive restored
opts.rat: Standard for opioid overdoses requiring multiple boluses, especially fentanyl, methadone, extended-release formulations. Prevents re-sedation while titration continues. Stop infusion gradually as opioid effect wanes.

opts.text: Bag-mask ventilation while preparing naloxone if RR <8 or apneic
opts.rat: Essential bridging intervention. Maintains oxygenation and ventilation while naloxone is being drawn up and administered. Continue BVM until respiratory drive returns. Have intubation equipment ready if drive does not recover.

opts.text: 0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus, then maintenance 200 mL/hr; target UOP >1 mL/kg/hr
opts.rat: Treats rhabdomyolysis (CK 4,200) and prevents myoglobin-induced AKI. Aggressive hydration is the cornerstone. Monitor for fluid overload given noncardiogenic pulmonary edema — balance is needed; use lung exam and SpO2 trends to guide volume.

opts.text: Continuous SpO2 and end-tidal CO2 (capnography) monitoring
opts.rat: Capnography is MORE sensitive than SpO2 for detecting hypoventilation. SpO2 stays normal until the client is already significantly desaturated. EtCO2 rises within seconds of decreased ventilation. Standard of care for monitoring post-overdose clients.

opts.text: Psychiatric and social work consultation; take-home naloxone (Narcan) prescription with education at discharge
opts.rat: Standard of care; reduces 12-month mortality after non-fatal overdose by ~50%. Education for the client AND household members (roommate) on overdose recognition and naloxone administration is critical.

opts.text: Buprenorphine bridge initiation in the ED with referral to outpatient MAT (medication-assisted treatment)
opts.rat: ED-initiated buprenorphine for opioid use disorder is now standard of care (recommended by ACEP, SAMHSA). Reduces 30-day mortality. Linkage to ongoing MAT (buprenorphine, methadone, or naltrexone) is the single highest-impact intervention for OUD.

opts.text: Empiric naloxone 2 mg IV PUSH (large initial bolus) regardless of clinical status
opts.rat: ⚠️ HIGH-YIELD NCLEX TRAP. Large bolus in an opioid-dependent client precipitates severe withdrawal — agitation, vomiting (aspiration risk), tachycardia, hypertension, possible seizures. Titration with small doses (0.04–0.4 mg) is preferred. Reserve 2 mg only for prehospital settings without IV access.

opts.text: Activated charcoal 50 g PO for opioid decontamination
opts.rat: Contraindicated. Ineffective for parenteral or already-absorbed opioids; in obtunded clients, aspiration risk is high. No role in opioid overdose management.

opts.text: Flumazenil 0.2 mg IV
opts.rat: Wrong reversal agent. Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine antagonist with no opioid activity. Adding it risks precipitating seizures if there is occult benzodiazepine dependence. Stick to naloxone.

opts.text: Discharge home immediately once the client is alert and conversational after the first naloxone dose
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS. Re-sedation risk extends 4–24+ hours depending on the opioid involved. Minimum 4–6 hr observation post-last-naloxone for short-acting opioids; 12–24 hr for fentanyl; 48 hr for methadone or extended-release. Premature discharge has caused fatal at-home re-sedations.

opts.text: Physical restraints if the client wakes up agitated from naloxone-precipitated withdrawal
opts.rat: Inappropriate as a first response. Treat the withdrawal symptoms (slow naloxone titration, antiemetics, IV fluids, clonidine for autonomic symptoms, calm environment). Restraints are a last resort for safety only after de-escalation fails and there's imminent harm risk.

opts.text: Use of stigmatizing terms ('addict,' 'junkie,' 'frequent flyer') in documentation or with the client
opts.rat: Unprofessional and counter-therapeutic. NCSBN, ACEP, AMA, and SAMHSA all explicitly endorse person-first, clinically accurate language ('person with opioid use disorder,' 'positive UDS,' 'substance use'). Stigmatizing language reduces treatment engagement and worsens outcomes.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Open airway, position client, provide supplemental O2 via NRB; begin bag-mask ventilation if RR <8 or apneic
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Airway/Breathing (ABCs). Always come before drugs. Maintains oxygenation and ventilation while preparing the reversal agent. Even brief hypoxia can cause irreversible brain injury.

opts.text: Administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV push; titrate with repeat 0.4 mg q2–3 min to RR >12 and adequate oxygenation
opts.rat: #2 — Immediate. The reversal agent that will restore respiratory drive. Titrate to respiratory drive, NOT to full alertness. Be prepared for the client to remain somnolent — that's fine if breathing is adequate.

opts.text: Confirm IV access; attach continuous capnography and SpO2 monitoring; obtain stat labs including ABG, BMP, CK, troponin, UDS, APAP, salicylate, glucose
opts.rat: #3 — Concurrent. IV access enables medication delivery; capnography catches hypoventilation before SpO2 changes. Labs identify co-ingestions, evaluate rhabdomyolysis, rule out other causes of altered mental status (hypoglycemia, acetaminophen toxicity).

opts.text: If respiratory depression recurs after 2–3 boluses: initiate naloxone IV infusion at ~2/3 of cumulative effective bolus dose per hour; start IV fluids 1 L NS bolus then 200 mL/hr for rhabdo prevention
opts.rat: #4 — Sustained therapy. Bolus + infusion bridges through the duration of the opioid effect, particularly important with fentanyl. Hydration prevents AKI from myoglobinuria.

opts.text: Once medically stable: psychiatric consult, social work referral, ED-initiated buprenorphine bridge with outpatient MAT linkage, take-home naloxone education for client and roommate
opts.rat: #5 — Disposition phase. After the acute crisis. These interventions reduce 12-month mortality by ~50% and are now standard of care. Don't skip them — they're the highest-impact disposition step.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After titrated naloxone 0.4 mg x2 and infusion at 0.4 mg/hr: RR 16, SpO2 96% on 2L NC, pupils 4 mm reactive, alert and oriented x4, no withdrawal symptoms
opts.rat: TEXTBOOK successful titration. Respiratory drive restored without precipitating withdrawal. Continue infusion and monitor for the appropriate observation window (12+ hours for fentanyl). Cooperative engagement allows for psychosocial intervention to begin.

opts.text: 6 hours post-arrival: weaning off naloxone infusion, CK trending down to 1,800, adequate UOP, repeat CXR shows resolving infiltrates, accepts buprenorphine bridge and take-home naloxone
opts.rat: Across-the-board recovery. Rhabdomyolysis resolving with hydration. Pulmonary edema clearing. Most importantly: engagement with MAT and harm reduction. This is the highest-impact moment for changing the trajectory of opioid use disorder.

opts.text: Discharged 24 hours after last naloxone dose with: take-home Narcan x2 packs, buprenorphine prescription for 7 days, scheduled appointment at addiction medicine clinic in 48 hours, roommate trained in naloxone use
opts.rat: Optimal disposition. ED-to-MAT bridging programs cut 30-day mortality dramatically. Take-home naloxone has well-established mortality benefit. Roommate education multiplies the safety net.

opts.text: RR drops to 4/min with apnea despite 3 doses of naloxone; SpO2 78% on NRB; client emergently intubated and placed on mechanical ventilation
opts.rat: Refractory respiratory depression or massive opioid load (often illicit fentanyl analogues like carfentanil). Continue naloxone infusion at higher rate. Mechanical ventilation provides definitive airway protection. ICU admission. Monitor for anoxic brain injury given prolonged hypoxia.

opts.text: After 2 mg IV bolus naloxone (rather than titrated dose): client awakens combative, vomits, aspirates, RR 32 with rhonchi, severe hypertension 198/118, brief generalized seizure
opts.rat: Naloxone-precipitated severe withdrawal with aspiration — preventable iatrogenic harm. ⚠️ The titration principle exists precisely to prevent this. Stop further naloxone if not needed for respiratory drive; supportive care (suction airway, antiemetics, IV fluids, benzodiazepine for seizure recurrence), and reassess respiratory status.

opts.text: CK climbs to 28,000, K+ 6.4, anuric for 4 hours despite hydration, creatinine rises to 4.2 — nephrology consulted for emergent CRRT
opts.rat: Severe rhabdomyolysis with AKI requiring renal replacement therapy. Worse prognosis but recoverable. CRRT clears myoglobin, potassium, and metabolic waste. Continue aggressive supportive care; renal function often recovers over weeks.

opts.text: Pulmonary edema progresses: bilateral white-out on CXR, PaO2/FiO2 ratio <100 despite high PEEP — meets ARDS criteria
opts.rat: Severe ARDS, likely from combined opioid-induced and naloxone-precipitated pulmonary edema. Manage with lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg tidal volume, plateau pressure <30, permissive hypercapnia), prone positioning, careful fluid management. ICU mortality remains significant.

opts.text: Client refuses follow-up, declines buprenorphine, leaves AMA after 12-hour observation
opts.rat: Common scenario. Even so, take-home naloxone, roommate education, and harm reduction information should be provided. Document the conversation, the offer of MAT, and that the client has the capacity to leave. Recurrence and mortality are high — do not give up on subsequent encounters.

opts.text: Mild residual sedation requiring continued capnography monitoring; periodic small boluses of naloxone needed every 2 hours through hour 10
opts.rat: Expected with fentanyl-positive overdoses. Continued monitoring through the appropriate observation window (12–24 hours for fentanyl) catches re-sedation episodes promptly. Wean naloxone infusion only when 4+ hours have passed without recurrence of respiratory depression.

opts.text: Roommate present at bedside, distressed but engaged in safety teaching; verbalizes understanding of naloxone use and overdose recognition
opts.rat: Expected and important. The roommate is also a person experiencing acute distress and needs support. Their education is one of the most concrete protective factors going forward. Family/household-trained naloxone has saved many lives.
